issues.title: Update Figure 1 to separate the two use cases of OTN-SC
issues.author: aguoietf
issues.body:
Use case 1: OTN-SC interfaces directly with orchestrator/e2e slice controller to provide OTN slice configuration for customers that directly request OTN resources.
Use case 2: OTN-SC interfaces with IETF NSC to provide OTN slice configuration that supports an IETF network slice within the OTN domain. 

OTN-SC NBI is technology specific, NSC-NBI is technology-agnostic. CMI is either tech-specific or tech-agnostic depending on the customer service

OTN-SC may be outside or inside of a PNC/MDSC but it does not affect its functionalities. Suggest to include both OTN-SC and PNC/MDSC in a box in the figure drawing.

issues.title: Use time slots vs. type/num of ODU containers to define OTN slice resources at the MPI
issues.author: aguoietf
issues.body:
For the NB of OTN-SC it is necessary to use type/number of ODU container from a high level abstraction. 

For the SB of OTN-SC (MPI of PNC/MDSC) it is unclear whether to use time slots or type/number of ODU container to reserve OTN slice resources in an abstract TE topology. Both time slots availability and ODU container availability are advertised in the TE topology given by a PNC/MDSC to the OTN-SC.

There are two potential use cases for time-slot based OTN slice resource reservation:
  - when an operator (who provides OTN slices to its customer) manages OTN resources by time slot numbers
  - for OTN inter-domain (high-order OTN-SC interfaces with two low-order OTN-SC, one for each domain, or high-order OTN-SC interfaces with two PNCs), it is necessary for the high-order OTN-SC to specify exact time slots for the inter-domain OTN link.

Options are: 
1. Use time slots
       +--rw (otn-slice-granularity)?
          +--:(link)
          |  +--rw slice-id?   uint32
          +--:(link-resource)
             +--rw slices* [slice-id]
                +--rw slice-id            uint32
                +--rw (technology)?
                |  +--:(otn)
                |     +--rw otn-ts-num?   uint32
                |     +--rw ts-list?      string

2. Use type/number of ODU container

3. Both

issues.title: YANG model for OTN-SC NBI
issues.author: aguoietf
issues.body:
Meeting minutes from 10-01-2021 related to OTN-SC NBI modeling:

We discussed an initial proposal for OTN-SC NBI which is targeted to merge vn-yang, draft-liu and possibly teas-ns into a single base model and augment it to create the OTN-SC NBI model. In the proposal:
-	We use vn + draft-liu topology as the basis of the base model
-	Connectivity matrix and SLO previously expressed in terms of TE constraints are moved directly to the model, thus separating vn from the TE topo. Clarified that connectivity matrix defined for vn/slice represents intended connections from customers whereas the connectivity matrix in TE represents potential connectivity.
-	It is possible to define multiple vn/slices with each having one connectivity matrix, or one vn/slice having multiple connectivity matrices. A relevant question is at what level is SLO defined, which kind of determines the cardinality above.
-	Whether it is “AP” or “VN-AP” that represents a slice endpoint is unclear. The teas-ns model seems to suggest an VN-AP equivalent entity, e.g. VLAN ID is used as a virtual slice endpoint, however it requires the VLAN ID of the slice endpoint to be pre-negotiated and pre-configured between the customer and provider ,which is not scalable and realistic. More clarification is needed on this point.
-	The network topology is suggested to be moved under the vn/slice as opposed to be under the root of the model.

We discussed the ongoing mailing list discussion regarding teas network slicing, in which 
-	It is unclear whether the teas-ns is attempting to include technology-specific traffic specification as defined in e.g. L3VPN, L2VPN, within the same network slice configuration. 
-	It is unclear at which level is the SLO, and whether multiple connectivity matrices are need and how the traffic is forwarded using a specific connectivity matrix. 
-	It is unclear whether the realization architecture and the use of “filter topology” is controller internal and does not need to be exposed by the slice controller.  

Please feel free to add your comments. Discussion slides for today are attached and are also uploaded to github.

comments.author: aguoietf
comments.body:
It was proposed on the last meeting (10/07/2021) that we create a base model for transport network slicing, which can be augmented for OTN, WDM and other technology-specific slicing in the transport network domain. 

Below is a draft proposal of such model (named as ietf-transport-network-slicing). Note that the network topology container is recreated based on the draft-liu model by copying all of the constructs from draft-liu, as I was unsure how draft-liu could be somehow "copied" or schema mounted. 
Also, the schema mount RFC (RFC8528) indicated that it supports only run-time or server-implemented mounting and does not support design-time mounting, which means it may be not suitable for this scenario.

Please have a look at the proposal and share your comments. 

```
module: ietf-transport-network-slice
  +--rw access-points
  |  +--rw access-point* [ap-id]
  |     +--rw ap-id         string
  |     +--rw pe-node-id?   string
  +--rw network-slices
     +--rw network-slice* [ns-id]
        +--rw ns-id                    string
        +--rw ns-description?          string
        +--rw customer-name*           string
        +--rw slo
        |  +--rw optimization-criterion?   identityref
        |  +--rw delay-tolerance?          boolean
        |  +--rw periodicity*              uint64
        |  +--rw isolation-level?          identityref
        +--rw endpoints
        |  +--rw endpoint* [endpoint-id]
        |     +--rw endpoint-id    string
        |     +--rw ap-id?         -> /access-points/access-point/ap-id
        +--rw network-topology
        |  +--rw topology* [topology-id]
        |     +--rw topology-id    string
        |     +--rw node* [node-id]
        |     |  +--rw node-id              inet:uri
        |     |  +--rw slo
        |     |  |  +--rw isolation-level?   identityref
        |     |  +--rw termination-point* [tp-id]
        |     |     +--rw tp-id          inet:uri
        |     |     +--rw endpoint-id?   -> /network-slices/network-slice/endpoints/endpoint/endpoint-id
        |     +--rw link* [link-id]
        |        +--rw link-id        inet:uri
        |        +--rw slo
        |        |  +--rw delay-tolerance?   boolean
        |        |  +--rw periodicity*       uint64
        |        |  +--rw isolation-level?   identityref
        |        +--rw source
        |        |  +--rw source-node?   -> ../../../node/node-id
        |        |  +--rw source-tp?     -> ../../../node[node-id=current()/../source-node]/termination-point/tp-id
        |        +--rw destination
        |           +--rw dest-node?   -> ../../../node/node-id
        |           +--rw dest-tp?     -> ../../../node[node-id=current()/../dest-node]/termination-point/tp-id
        +--rw connectivity-matrices
           +--rw connectivity-matrix* [connectivity-matrix-id]
              +--rw connectivity-matrix-id    uint32
              +--rw topology-id?              -> /network-slices/network-slice/network-topology/topology/topology-id
              +--rw src-endpoint?             -> ../../../endpoints/endpoint/endpoint-id
              +--rw dst-endpoint?             -> ../../../endpoints/endpoint/endpoint-id
              +--rw slo
              +--rw underlay-path* [tp-id]
                 +--rw tp-id    -> /network-slices/network-slice/network-topology/topology/node/termination-point/tp-id
```

comments.author: italobusi
comments.body:
I need more time to understand in details the slo attributes but the overall model looks going in the right direction

Few minor detailed comments:

1. Do we need the `pe-node-id` attribute?
2. Why the `customer-name` is a list and not a single attribute?
3. I will add a `ns-name` attribute in addition to `ns-id` and `ns-description` (like in other models)
4. Do we need a container to contain a list? I have seen some models written with this approach and other models not following this approach but I do not understand the rationale for the different choices
   1. If the external container is needed, should it be `network-topologies` instead of `network-topology`?
5. I think that the `topology-id` should use a relative path, otherwise the `ns-id` is missing
6. I think that the `tp-id` should also use a relative path, otherwise the `ns-id` is missing. 
   1. The topology-id should be specified: `network-topology/topology[topology-id=../../topology-id]/`
   2. Also the `node-id` should be specified: `node[node-id=../node-id]/termination-point`
3.  instead of `underlay-path` to avoid misunderstanding?

comments.author: aguoietf
comments.body:
Are you considering the case where we make an access point an endpoint and keep a list of endpoints outside of the network slice configuration, and the network slice specifies endpoints by leafref to the list? In this case, yes endpoints in the list is rw but only because of YANG leafref limitation.

However, I thought that at the end it was said that there is no need to keep a separate list of access points in such case - endpoints are pre-negotiated so both the customer and provider knows exactly what an endpoint refers to.  Let me know if this is a different understanding. 

Also, yes an explicit path means a path within the network topology, and it is equivalent to the primary path in the VN model. Since VN uses TE topology as its underlay and here we bring the topology into this model without an underlay, I'd think it is better to not call it an underlay but rather a path within the topology. What do you think?

comments.author: aguoietf
comments.body:
@italobusi 
Raw list of the comments I have taken yesterday:
Is the list of match criteria represents and OR or an AND?
Example: VLAN 100 and MPLS label 50

Duplication of information (e.g., VLAN value, SDP peering) between SDP and AC ...
Why the SDP role and the connectivity-construct-id is defined per match-criteria and not per SDP

@sergiobelotti 
Originally SDP role was under SDP (SAP originally) but the intention is to permit SDP to have different roles depending of participation of different connection.constructs (groups). Matching criteria in SDP, are pointing to target-connectivity group id and connection-group-sdp-role

@italobusi As discussed today, this is not always possible with the current model structure

 

For example, it is not possible to define an SDP for VLAN 100 and 200 and associate VLAN 100 to a connectivity construct (e.g., with low latency) and VLAN 200 to another connectivity construct (e.g., with high bandwidth) this is due to the fact that the tag-type (e.g., VLAN in this case) is the key of the list so it is not possible to have two entries with the same key pointing to two different connectivity constructs

 

It is also not fully clear to me also how it is possible to define an SDP for VLAN 100 and two connectivity constructs associated based on the DSCP field

issues.title: Collection of mailing list comments during the WG adoption call
issues.author: aguoietf

comments.author: aguoietf
comments.body:
Some notes taken from the call with Igor on 02/07/22:

Igor: slicing was defined starting at the IP layer
Italo: TEAS extended slicing beyond 5G
Sergio: TEAS design team confirmed that 5G is one use case for slicing but more use cases can be called as slicing
Igor/all: from 5G perspective there is no difference between IP and OTN. 
Igor: Slicing should be used only for 5G. 
Igor: there were "OTN slices" before 2016. Network virtualization was a hot term at that time. "Slice" was introduced in Q12 but later replaced with virtual networks. There is no problem with the term "slice" except that it conflate
Italo: intent vs. realization. Drafts in TEAS (VPN+, etc.) are introduced to realize the intent for network slicing. 
Igor: framework is still work in progress, what is SLO? 
Italo: OTN can still have failures there is a need for SLO definitions.
Italo: 10G slice in packet can change bandwidth / Aihua: so is OTN with flexible ODU
Haomian: Q12 did not talk about IETF NS. Conclusion is 3GPP slicing should not directly be applied to using IETF NS. What has been specified as "VN" by G.7702 is sufficient for ITU.
Igor: L1VPN, OTN topology, VN which is NBI and resembles OTN slicing. 
Italo/Sergio: The same discussion happened in TEAS. Arguments that were rejected by TEAS is that "VN" and other terms can be reused. 
Italo: VN is technology-agnostic and we got strong push from TEAS that ACTN applies to all TE technologies including IP and OTN. Decision is taken by TEAS. 
Igor: Slicing is a technology-agnostic concept. The word "slicing" is common.
Italo: NS framework did not specify packet marking or marking OTN time slots. 
Italo: at the NBI there is no difference of TE tunnel seeing in-band or out-of-band control plane communication. 
Italo: OTN slicing describes a NBI object not data plane
Sergio: The comments about "slicing" should be addressed in the NS framework and TEAS
Igor: Work is in progress and premature.
Italo: VN is not a piece of physical network
Italo: there is currently no intent-like interface that can be used by OTN-aware orchestrator
Igor: for multi-layer slice the orchestrator can use TE topology for network slicing. 
Italo: why this concept is not established by TEAS?
Luis: Agree that OTN is simple to realize SLO. Additionally,
   the IETF Network Slice customer might ask for some level of control
   of their virtual networks, e.g., to customize the service paths in a
   network slice.
Haomian: there are other parameter that are OTN oriented used for slicing. 
Igor: no problem to define using resource allocation. Just a problem with the term slicing.
Italo: do we agree that the term slicing used by OTN is consistent with the current framework document? 
Igor: slice is a logical network - agreed. 
Haomian: TEAS NS framework defined a slot of SLx which can be applicable to OTN. We can construct an OTN slicing model based on that. 
Igor: slicing is not a good term in general 
Igor: sufficient to indicate that we are open to discuss alternative terms.
Haomian: if TEAS NS is for IP only then it should be moved to other WG. 
Sergio: TEAS NS is independent of underlay network. We are mixing what is intent and what is realization. 
Igor: VN is what OTN slicing is. 
Italo: VN is technology agnostic and applies to teas NS. The argument should go to TEAS.
Igor: framework defines mp2mp connectivity 
Igor: has a problem with term IETF slice in general, could use a better term.

issues.title: Update the text to clarify OTN-SC's role as a NS-realizer w.r.t. IETF NSC
issues.author: aguoietf
issues.body:
[draft-contreras-teas-slice-controller-models](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-contreras-teas-slice-controller-models/) defines the structure of an IETF NSC as:

~~~
                                    Higher Level System
                                             |
                                             |
                                ---------------------------
                                | NSC        | (a)        |
                                |            v            |
                                |   -------------------   |
                                |   |                 |   |
                                |   |    NS Mapper    |   |
                                |   |                 |   |
                                |   -------------------   |
                                |            | (b)        |
                                |            v            |
                                |   -------------------   |
                                |   |                 |   |
                                |   |    NS Realizer  |   |
                                |   |                 |   |
                                |   -------------------   |
                                |            | (c)        |
                                ---------------------------
                                             |
                                             v
                                    Network Controllers

     Figure 2: IETF Network Slice Controller structure and asspociated
                                data models
~~~

OTN-SC Diagram:
~~~
                         +--------------------+
                         | Provider's User    |
                         +--------|-----------+
                                  | CMI
          +-----------------------+----------------------------+
          |          Orchestrator / E2E Slice Controller       |
          +------------+-----------------------------+---------+
                       |                             | NSC-NBI
                       |       +---------------------+---------+
                       |       | IETF Network Slice Controller |
                       |       +-----+---------------+---------+
                       |             |               |
                       | OTN-SC NBI  |OTN-SC NBI     |
          +------------+-------------+--------+      |
          |               OTN-SC              |      |
          +--------------------------+--------+      |
                                     | MPI           | MPI
          +--------------------------+---------------+---------+
          |                         PNC                        |
          +--------------------------+-------------------------+
                                     | SBI
                         +-----------+----------+
                         |OTN Physical Network  |
                         +----------------------+

              Figure 2: Positioning of OTN Slicing Interfaces
~~~
It would be helpful for the draft to reflect the fact that OTN-SC is just a “NS Realizer” for OTN network. A possible update is:
- From the IETF NSC point of view, OTN-SC can be seen as an NS realizer.
- From the view of an OTN-aware orchestrator, OTN-SC could server as both an (OTN-)NS mapper and an NS realizer.

[mail thread](https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/ccamp/60IqKlttsTxb7q3Mi-iTh6I5mjc/)

comments.author: aguoietf
comments.body: In the context of [draft-contreras-teas-slice-controller-models](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-contreras-teas-slice-controller-models/). NS mapper is to map a technology-agnostic request to a technology-specific request. Therefore, an OTN-SC can be viewed as a NS realizer.

comments.author: aguoietf
comments.body:
text proposal:

~~~~
   The presence of OTN-SC provides multiple options for a high-level slice controller 
   or an orchestrator to configure and realize slicing in OTN networks, depending on
   whether a customer's slice request is technology agnostic or technology specific:
    
   Option 1[opt.1]: An IETF NSC receives a technology-agnostic slice request from the IETF NSC NBI and
   realizes full or part of the slice in OTN networks directly through MPI provided by
   the PNC or MDSC. The IETF NSC is responsible for mapping a technology-agnostic slicing request 
   into an OTN technology-specific realization. In this option, the OTN-SC is not used.
   
   Option 2[opt.2]: An IETF NSC receives a technology-agnostic slice request from the IETF NSC NBI and delegates the
   request to the OTN-SC through the OTN-SC NBI, which is OTN technology specific. The OTN-SC in turn realizes the 
slice in single or multi domain OTN networks by working with the underlying PNC or MDSC. In this option, the OTN-SC is 
considered as a realization of IETF NSC, i.e., an NS realizer as per 
{{!I-D.draft-contreras-teas-slice-controller-models}},
   when the underlying network is OTN. The OTN-SC is also a subordinate slice controller of the IETF NSC, which 
   is consistent with the hierarchical control of slices defined by the IETF network slice framework.
   
   Option 3[opt.3]: An OTN-aware orchestrator may request an OTN technology-specific slice with OTN-specific SLOs 
through the OTN-SC NBI to the OTN-SC. The OTN-SC in turn realizes the slice in single or multi domain OTN 
networks by working with the underlying PNC or MDSC
   
   An OTN slice may be realized by using standard MPI interfaces, control plane,     
   network management system (NMS) or any other proprietary interfaces as needed. Examples of such
   interfaces include the abstract TE topology {{!RFC8795}}, TE tunnel {{!I-D.ietf-teas-yang-te}},
   L1VPN{{!RFC4847}}, or Netconf/YANG based interfaces such as OpenConfig. Some of these interfaces,
   such as the TE tunnel model, are suitable for creating connectivity-based OTN slices which represent a 
   slice as a set of TE tunnels, while other interfaces such as the TE topology model are more suitable for 
   creating resource-based OTN slices which represent a slice as a topology.
   
   The OTN-SC NBI is a technology-specific interface that augments the IETF NSC NBI, which is technology-
   agnostic. 
   
   {{fig-slice-interfaces}} illustrates the OTN slicing control hierarchy 
   , the positioning of the OTN slicing interfaces as well as the options for OTN slice configuration.


~~~~
                      +--------------------+
                      | Provider's User    |
                      +--------|-----------+
                               | CMI
       +-----------------------+----------------------------+
       |          Orchestrator / E2E Slice Controller       | 
       +------------+-----------------------------+---------+
                    |                             | NSC-NBI
                    |       +---------------------+---------+
                    |       | IETF Network Slice Controller |
                    |       +-----+---------------+---------+
                    | opt.3       | opt.2         | opt.1
                    | OTN-SC NBI  |OTN-SC NBI     |               
       +------------+-------------+--------+      |
       |               OTN-SC              |      |
       +--------------------------+--------+      |      
                                  | MPI           | MPI                           
       +--------------------------+---------------+---------+ 
       |                         PNC                        | 
       +--------------------------+-------------------------+ 
                                  | SBI
                      +-----------+----------+
                      |OTN Physical Network  |
                      +----------------------+

~~~~

issues.title: Relationship between an OTN slice and L1VPN
issues.author: aguoietf
issues.body:
Clarify the relationship between an OTN slice and L1VPN
- Is it the case that OTN slice is an intent-like interface at the NBI, and L1VPN is a possible realization of an OTN slice
- Can an OTN slice be realized by multiple L1VPNs, e.g. a cardinality of 1:N between an OTN slice and a L1VPN.

[mail thread](https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/ccamp/l1EDpQ3eIsW86sBETWggeNrQDQ4/)

comments.author: aguoietf
comments.body:
Igor's comments:

> Remember, we have this thing called IP VPN?  Network slice is roughly IP VPN with QOS parameters. 
> Considering that L1VPN cannot be without QOS, my question to the authors what is the difference between L1VPN and OTN slice?
> 
> Firstly, I still can't see a daylight between L1VPN and OTN slice. I'd also like to hear from the authors something more than L1VPN is one way to implement OTN slice. What would be other ways? And what about  L0? Would we also need OCh slicing, microwave slicing, etc.? I think such a discussion would be welcome addition to the draft.
> 
> Secondly, we put a lot of effort into L1VPNs - we had a dedicated WG, which successfully fulfilled its charter and was closed with honors. Looking back though, I hope you'd agree that the level of L1VPN success/deployments, to put it mildly, has not been as high, as we had hoped it would be.  In your opinion why is that? Could it be that because over the years folks realized it is far simpler and straightforward (yet sufficient) to plan and use OTN resources in a shared manner, providing simple p2p connectivity services, rather than breaking the resources into VPNs and providing more complex L1VPN services?
> 
> Finally, I have a hypothetical question for you. Imagine that the IP layer data plane was naturally sliced as OTN data plane. For example, imagine IP layer where the only way to achieve any connectivity is via RSVP TE controlled LSPs, which have impenetrable walls separating them from each other (regardless whether you want such walls or not). Do you think we would be working on slicing in such IP?


Aihua's comments

> IMHO an OTN slice, like an IETF network slice, is used to represent an intent from a (internal or external) customer's view. This intent includes a combination of resource requirements which can be expressed by connectivities or topologies, along with the common and OTN technology-specific SLOs. Customers usually do not care how an OTN slice is realized at the control plane and/or data plane, and VPN is one of the possible realizations. 
>  
> To summarize, an L1VPN with QoS parameters can be considered as a possible realization of an OTN slice. 
>  
> Also, if I understand it correctly, L1VPN is implemented using the control plane per rfc4878 and is not targeted as an NBI model. 


Adrian's comments

> I think there is a difference between a L3/L2 VPN and a L1VPN that is to do with the nature of connectivity in the underlying network. A packet VPN is expressed as a set of edge points and the edge connectivity capacities, but not the edge-to-edge capacities. A L1VPN (at least for some L1 technologies – but not all?) has to be expressed in terms of edge-to-edge capacities because you cannot do “on-demand” traffic routing within the network – the flow markings are at a macro level.
> 
> _>From that perspective, I’d agree that L1VPN is close to L1 slicing. It would be good to hear the authors spell out the differences that they obviously have in mind._
> 
> But, not only did Daniele ask his question in TEAS, he also go an answer 😊. There are service models in which a network slice may look like a “VPN with QoS” – those are the enhanced VPN cases. But there are also service models where the edge-to-edge flows are spelled out in more detail than in a VPN.
> 

Dianele's comments

> The distinction between realization and intention makes sense. I believe that from the realization point of view they are identical (control plane excluded) but from an intent point of view there is a difference.


Italo's comments

> This is also my understanding.
> 
> Igor is right that from a data plane perspective all the “ingredients” to realize an OTN slice are defined.
> 
> Section 3 of the draft describes the framework for OTN slicing and identifies few gaps in the YANG data models. No gaps are identified in the data plane.
> 
> Section 4 of the draft proposes new YANG data models to address the gaps identified in section 3.
>

comments.author: sergiobelotti
comments.body:
I suggest this piece of text , to be put just at the beginning of section 3 .

"An IETF Network Slice Service interface is permitting a customer to express requirements for a particular slice specifying what is required and not how this can be achieved. This is typical characteristic of slicing in which the customer view of a slice is abstract and based on a logical abstract topology. 
Abstract topology is defined in RFC8453, as composed by abstract node and abstract link as defined in RFC 7926, providing allocation of resources and being able to convey  to the client characteristics related to the logical network helping client to make proper TE decision , e.g for path computation. 
Network slice includes abstract topology service adding the capability to steer the performance network indicator (SLI) towards the committed SLOs permitting  the client to use logical network as a stable and reliable physical network."

issues.title: Applicability of NRP to OTN slicing
issues.author: italobusi
issues.body:
NRP concept is introduced in section 6 of https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-teas-ietf-network-slices-10 to support the realization of IETF network slices

At the first glance, the concept looks similar to the "marking" of OTN resources described in section 4.1 of https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-ccamp-yang-otn-slicing-01

However, further investigation is required to understand whether NRP is a mandatory construct for IETF network slice realization and whether the NRP concept really matches the description in section 4.1 of https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-ccamp-yang-otn-slicing-01, in particular considering other YANG models being proposed in TEAS WG for the NRP YANG models

comments.author: italobusi
comments.body:
This is the list of drafts in TEAS WG dealing with the NRP YANG model:

- https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-wd-teas-nrp-yang-00: this draft describes a YANG model for NRP and clearly states that it is a network configuration model (thus applicable between the NSC and the underlay network controller)
- https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-bestbar-teas-yang-nrp-policy-00: this draft describes a YANG model to configure the NRP policies to be used to instantiate NRP:
  - The applicability of this YANG model is not very clear but it references https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-bestbar-teas-ns-packet-08
  - The NRP policy depends on the configuration of topology filters defined in https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-bestbar-teas-yang-topology-filter-03

comments.author: aguoietf
comments.body:
According to the discussion today here are my thoughts regarding NRP for OTN slicing:

NRP (Network Resource Partition) is a construct internal to the NSC to facilitate the mapping of a network slice to the underlay network. An NRP is a collection of resources identified in the underlay network and, although not required, may be represented as a network topology. The relationship between network slices and NRPs is M:N, meaning that multiple network slices can be created within the same NRP, and different connectivity constructs of a network slice can be mapped to separate NRPs.

An NRP may be used to address the scalability issue in packet networks, where there will be large amount of control and data plane states that need to be stored and programmed in a router if network slices are mapped directly to the router.  OTN resources are much more coarse granular (current ODU time slot granularity is ODU0~ODUCn, with the possibility to go lower in the future for sub-1G) and resources are usually abstracted within a single topology for a single OTN domain. The Use of NRP for OTN slicing is therefore neither eminent nor mandatory. 

There are a few options for an OTN-SC to use (or not use) NRP:
The OTN-SC may map an OTN slice directly onto the underlay TE topology received from the PNC. 
The OTN-SC may map an OTN slice to an NRP, which is 1:1 mapped to the underlay TE topology received from the PNC.
The OTN-SC may map an OTN slice to an NRP, which is an abstract TE topology created on top of the underlay TE topology. In this case there can be multiple NRPs (abstract TE topologies) created from the underlay TE topology.

In either of the options, the same MPI interface defined in this draft can be used at the OTN-SC SBI (MPI).

comments.author: sergiobelotti
comments.body:
> I agree on most of your comment. I would add that the relationship between connectivity-construct and NRP resources in N:1 , and that a single connectivity construct is mapped to only one NRP. In the definition of NRP is also present the filter-topology that in my view is producing an abstract  virtual topology as we have in section 4.1.1 in our draft.
Following this thought I think the the third option you describe for OTN-SC would be perfect in line with filter topology usage of figure 4 of https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-teas-ietf-network-slices-10
So I do not think we should make particular actions with respect NRP in our OTN context.

comments.author: aguoietf
comments.body:
Text proposal for NRP descriptions and diagrams:


4. Realizing OTN Slices

>[I-D.ietf-teas-ietf-network-slices] introduces a mechanism for an IETF network slice controller to realize network slices by constructing Network Resource Partitions (NRP). A NRP is a collection of resources identified in the underlay network to facilitate the mapping of network slices onto available network resources. An NRP is a scope view of a topology and may be considered as a topology in its own right. Thus, in traffic-engineered (TE) networks including OTN, an NRP may be simply represented as an abstract TE tology defined by [RFC8795]. For OTN networks, An NRP may be respresented as an abstract OTN topology defined by [I-D.ietf-ccamp-otn-topo-yang]. 

>The NRP may be used to address the scalability issues where there may be considerable numbers of control and data plane states required to be stored and programmed if network slices are mapped directly to the underlay topology. NRP is internal to a network slice controller, and use of NRPs is optional yet could benefit a network slice realization in large-scale networks, including OTN networks. 

>Without using the NRP, the OTN-SC may map an OTN slice directly onto the underlay TE topology presented by the subtended network controller (MDSC or PNC). In this case the OTN-SC needs to color corresponding link resources of the underlay topology with a slice identififer and maintain the coloring in order to keep track of the mapping of OTN slices. The OTN-SC pushes the colored topology to the subtended MDSC or PNC using the MPI model defined in this draft.

>Alternatively, by using the NRP, an OTN slice is mapped to a NRP as an overlay abstract OTN TE topology on top of the underlay topology. The corresponding link resources allocated to the slice is encapsulated in and tracked using the abstract topology, thus eliminating the need of coloring links in the underlay topology. Multiple OTN slices may be mapped to the same NRP, and a single connectivity construct of the slice may be mapped to only one NRP, as per [I-D.ietf-teas-ietf-network-slices].

issues.title: Define customized topology
issues.author: aguoietf
issues.body:
In the previous meetings between network slicing and OTN slicing, we agreed that:
- An NS topology is a customer's intent
- A network slice (including an OTN slice) has references to a topology which is defined outside of the slice
- A topology should have SLO/SLE defined for its links, nodes and TPs. The SLO/SLEs defined for topology should be consistent with those defined for connections.
- Some SLO/SLE attributes == TE topology attributes
- Other SLO/SLE attributes are abstract and does not link to TE attributes

Possible option to define a customized topology:
- Remove ietf-transport-network-slice (tns) from the OTN slicing draft, and reuse [Xufeng's model for network slicing](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-liu-teas-transport-network-slice-yang-05)
- Reuse SLO/SLE attributes defined in IETF network slicing NBI. Migrate SLO/SLEs defined in ietf-transport-network-slice to Xufeng's model.

issues.author: sergiobelotti
issues.body:
In section 4 of the document the two alternatives methods to create resource-based OTN slices have been introduced but a better description is needed .
This is the piece of text to be improved:
```
`For resource-based OTN slices, the OTN-SC may map an OTN slice
   directly onto the underlay TE topology presented by the subtended
   network controller (MDSC or PNC) without creating NRP topologies.
   Due to the need for reserving resources, the OTN-SC needs to color
   corresponding link resources of the underlay topology with a slice
   identifier and maintain the coloring to keep track of the mapping of
   OTN slices.  The OTN-SC may push the colored topology to the
   subtended MDSC or PNC using the MPI model defined in this draft.

   Alternatively, an OTN slice may be mapped to a NRP as an overlay
   abstract OTN TE topology on top of the underlay topology.  The
   corresponding link resources allocated to the slice is encapsulated
   in and tracked by the abstract topology, and a given link or port in
   the NRP topology represents resources that are reserved in the
   underlay topology.  Multiple OTN slices may be mapped to the same
   NRP, and a single connectivity construct of the slice may be mapped
   to only one NRP, as per [RFC9543].  The resources of an NRP topology
   are reserved and shared by all the OTN slices mapped to this NRP, and
   the NRP topology may be pushed directly to the subtended MDSC or PNC,
   thus eliminating the need for link coloring if using the underlay
   topology.
```
`

issues.title: Support for hierarchical NRP
issues.author: aguoietf
issues.body:
draft-li-composite-slice proposes the solution for the realization of Multi-domain Network Slices and hierarchical network slices.

It is unclear if it is describing scenarios for hierarchical network slicing, or for hierarchical NRP

The solution presented in draft-ietf-ccamp-yang-otn-slicing supports multi-domain NRP and hierarchical slicing (see Fig. 3). However, hierarchical NRP (i.e. hierarchical NRP ids) is currently not supported.

Further clarification is needed from draft-li to determine if hierarchical NRP needs to be supported for also OTN slicing

comments.author: aguoietf
comments.body:
For connectivity-based OTN slices, NRP is optional - a connection within an OTN slice can be realized by an OTN tunnel in the underlay topology
For resource-based OTN slices, NRP is typically required due to the need for reserving resources - one OTN slice topology is typically realized by one dedicated NRP topology.

Need to add the above description in the document.

y using the NRP, an OTN slice is mapped to a NRP as an overlay abstract OTN TE topology on top of the underlay topology e.g. In other words, instead of saying that a given link in the underlay TE topology is reserved to a red slice, the OTN-SC creates a link within an NRP topology that reserves all the resources of a given link of underlay-topology (link-based partitioning). The same in case of TS-based portioning, instead of saying that some TSs on a given link in the underlay TE topology are reserved to a red slice, the OTN-SC creates a link within the red NRP topology that reserves some TSs on a given link in the underlay TE topology.

Alternatively, by using the NRP, an OTN slice is mapped to a NRP as an overlay abstract OTN TE topology on top of the underlay topology. The corresponding link resources allocated to the slice is encapsulated in and tracked by the abstract topology, and a given link or port in the NRP topology represents resources that are reserved in the underlay topology. The use of NRP eliminates the need of coloring links in the underlay topology. 

Multiple OTN slices may be mapped to the same NRP, and a single connectivity construct of the slice may be mapped to only one NRP, as per [I-D.ietf-teas-ietf-network-slices].

Editorial comment plus kramdown syntax issue

OLD

> For OTN networks, An NRP may be represented as an abstract OTN topology defined by [I-D.ietf-ccamp-otn-topo-yang]


NEW

> For OTN networks, an NRP may be represented as an abstract OTN topology defined by {{!I-D.ietf-ccamp-otn-topo-yang}}

I would align the description as the text below

OLD

> a connection within an OTN slice can be realized


NEW

> a connection within an OTN slice is typically realized

In the YANG model, it is possible to define request more than one topology for one resource-based OTN slice. In this case, I think we will have one NRP for each slice topology rather than for each resource-based OTN slice

OLD

> One OTN slice is typically realized by one dedicated NRP topology


NEW

> One slice topology for a resource-based OTN slice is typically realized by one dedicated NRP topology

comments.body:
"...instantiate TE tunnels, related to the underlay link resources,  with the appropriate NRP identifier".

Better to remind that each link is formed by an end-to-end tunnel across the underlying OTN networks.

In this model, the link resources allocated to the slice are encapsulated and tracked within the abstract NRP topology. Each link or port in the NRP topology corresponds to resources reserved in the underlay topology. 

I'm wondering if it is needed the first sentence: the second sentence is very clear, what is adding the first sentence? I have difficult to understand what does it mean that the link resources are "encapsulated and tracked" in an NRP topo. Maybe I missed something, but I would suggest to delete the sentence or reword a little.

When you talked about the "underlying virtual TE topology" you mean the topology exposed by MDSC/PNC to  OTN-SC? I would prefer to use the term underlying topology for the native topology, and maybe to use abstract TE topology for the one exposed by MDSC.
I'm wondering if it would be more appropriate to change the name of the grouping into "otn-nrp-rpofile" since the link is already a result of choice , alternative to link resource
Change "NRP identifier" into "NRP link resource identifier"
ok. rephrase slightly: "...and subsequently instantiate TE tunnels **utilizing the related underlay link resources** with the appropriate NRP identifier."

This was intended to express that the resources of an OTN slice is represented by an abstract TE topology built on top of the underlay topology.
How about updating the sentence to:
"In this model, the link resources allocated to the slice are represented by the abstract NRP topology, wherein each link or port in the NRP topology is mapped to resources reserved in the underlay topology"

ok. we could rephrase to : "underlying TE topology" in this case which is also aligned with the description next, "In this case, the slice is configured by marking the relevant link resources on the TE topology received from the MDSC or PNC..."
yes, I agree that ont-nrp-profile sounds better.
ok

I think we have agreed to model for OTN NRP are configured only by assigning the NRP-ID on the resources in the underlay topology and not by creating different NRP topology instances.

Before removing this sentence, we need to move some useful information here above:
- the fact that multiple OTN slices can be mapped to the same NRP
- the resources reserved for an NRP-ID are reserved and shared among all the OTN slides mapped to that NRP

I think we need also to review the figure below: if we do not have time, we can just add an editors' note and create an issue in github

In the first paragraph:
- I think we also need to mention the OTN tunnel model
- one change is needed to configure the NRP-ID associated to the OTN tunnel

For the second paragraph:
- I would separate it from the first one
- based on the latest discussion, I think this paragraph is describing the NRP configuration and not the OTN slicing configuration

For the **realization** of connectivity-based OTN slices, the OTN-SC configures an OTN tunnel using the OTN tunnel model with the NRP id.
For the realization of resource-based OTN slices, the OTN-SC configures an OTN NRP.  In this case, the NRP is configured by marking the relevant link resources on the TE topology received from the MDSC or PNC with an NRP identifier and OTN-specific resource attributes, such as the number of ODU time slots or the type and quantity of ODU containers.
